course_description:
This course involves reading texts that grow out of Europe's encounters with the non-European world and Indigenous peoples. These encounters have been a persistent concern of both European and particularly non-European writers. The course will involve texts that reflect on Europe's impact on the world, examine the legacy of colonial powers, show forms of cultural and political resistance to colonial governments, address issues of violent confrontation and exploitation, and explore processes of reconciliation. Through engagement with different literary genres and mediums in the texts of both European and non-European writers, students will explore conflicting representations of the West and its contested legacy outside Europe.

BAdvHum(Hons) students taking this course as an elective please email westernciv@uq.edu.au

Empires have shaped the world for thousands of years. The world may be politically divided into sovereign states, but the legacies of empire survive. This course will explore the literature of, about, and against empire from early modern times to the present. Divided into four parts, the course starts by analysing the relationship between liberalism and empire before returning to early modern European accounts of the non-European world in both fictional and non-fictional writing. The course then turns to forms of anti-colonial resistance and the critique of European representations of the non-European ‘other’. The final part of the course considers the way modern fiction and film have treated ethical and political issues of cultural encounters on the imperial frontier.

Part 1: Liberalism and Empire

Mill,ﾠ‘Civilisation’ (1836) and ‘A Few Words on Non-Intervention’ (1859)

Part 2: Early Modern Europe and the Other

Las Casas, A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies (1552)

Montaigne, ‘On Cannibals’ (1580)

Behn, Oroonoko (1688)ﾠ

Defoe, Robinson Crusoe (1719)

Part 3: Anti-Colonial Resistance

Fanon, Wretched of the Earth (1961)ﾠ

Said, Orientalism (1978)ﾠ

Part 4: Frontier Imaginaries

Conrad, Heart of Darkness (1902)ﾠ

Coppola, Apocalypse Now (1979)ﾠ

Scott, That Deadman Dance (2010)ﾠ

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:
restrictions: Restricted to students enrolled in the BAdvHum(Hons) and BHum/LLB(Hons). Maximum enrolment quota of 60 students.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand some of the central themes and concepts in Western thought on empire.
Appreciate the intellectual challenge of recovering what past authors meant by the things they wrote.
Recognise and understand the importance of conflict and struggle in the development of ideas.
Be aware of the historical reception and re-interpretation of imperial and colonial ideas.
Be able to position key ideas and texts in relation to diverse contexts and interests.
Understand the challenges and opportunities in studying contemporary colonial legacies.
Demonstrate an ability to communicate understanding of empire through modes of written and oral expression appropriate to the humanities disciplines.

assessment_details.title: Research Essay
assessment_details.task_description: Length: 2,000 words *A list of essay questions will be circulated during the semester. The essay should focus on the primary text , rather than secondary sources, but secondary sources should be consulted. Students are encouraged to use secondary sources in preparing their essay, but these MUST be selected from the WCIV2100 Further Readings list (found on Blackboard). If a student wishes to use secondary sources outside this list, permission must be sought from the course coordinator in advance (at least 48 hours before the due date) with justification for each source . Penalties will be applied for using secondary sources not on the WCIV2100 Further Readings list. **Note on AI use: While UQ in its wisdom prevents me from prohibiting AI use in assessments, I strongly encourage you to approach AI tools thoughtfully and limit their role in the preparation and writing of your essay . On submission of your video presentation, your essay, and any other written submissions for WCIV2100 you are required to fill out the course AI Declaration Coversheet . ** Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics will be available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.

assessment_details.title: Seminar participation
assessment_details.task_description: Students will be evaluated on the quality of contributions they make to seminar discussion. Your participation will be assessed according to the following criteria: 1. Evidence of participation in discussions. 2. Evidence of preparation for class, most notably through completing the set readings 3. Quality of contributions to discussions. 4. Fostering of group discussions. 5. Attendance is necessary to fulfill the above criteria, as per the course requirements. This assessment task evaluates students’ abilities, skills, and knowledge without the aid of Artificial Intelligence (AI). If students are unable to attend a seminar, they may submit a one-page summary-analysis of the text as a substitute for the missed class. On submission of your summary, you are required to fill out the AI Declaration Coversheet. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Video Presentation
assessment_details.task_description: Each student is required to make a video presentation (of no longer than 4 minutes ) on the relevance of a chosen text for understanding early European conceptions of empire. The presentation must focus on one of the following authors/texts: Mill's essays on 'Civilisation' and 'A Few Words on Non-intervention', Las Casas's A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies , Montaigne's 'On Cannibals', and Behn's Oroonoko , Defoe's Robinson Crusoe. UQ Library has prepared a useful guide on creating video presentations: https://elearning.uq.edu.au/student-guides-original/echovideo-original/submit-video-assignment-echovideo-original This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .

Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Oral Assessment
assessment_details.task_description: Task Description : This is a 10-minute individual oral examination designed to assess your understanding of course texts through spontaneous discussion. You will be scheduled for an individual meeting with the course coordinator. Text selection and reading : At the start of your oral assessment, you will nominate two texts (and passages from each text) from the course readings (excluding any text that is the focus of your research essay). The coordinator will select one of your nominated texts and ask you to read aloud a selected passage. Bring physical copies of both selected passages to your assessment (including page numbers in the prescribed course text) . Students should not bring any other printed matter to the oral assessment (eg., a prepared print-out of the opening statement or other extended notes). Opening statement : The coordinator will then ask you to provide a brief exegesis and explanation of the chosen passage. Follow-up discussion : Follow-up questions will be asked to help you expand, clarify, or deepen your initial response. The coordinator will also pose further questions, asking you to relate your selected passage and text to other texts studied in the course and to broader course-related themes and concepts. All questions are designed to give you opportunities to demonstrate your knowledge—they are not intended to trick or intimidate you. The oral assessment will be recorded for assessment purposes (audio only). Preparation : Review all course texts thoroughly, focusing on key arguments, themes, and concepts. Select two texts as the starting point of your oral assessment (excluding the subject of your essay). Choose a specific passage from each text that you consider significant for understanding politics or a specific political concept or argument. Practice articulating your thoughts about texts clearly and concisely. Bring physical copies of both selected passages to your assessment Be prepared to discuss any text, including the one you wrote about in your research essay Assessment Criteria: Content (Knowledge & Analysis): Completeness and accuracy of responses Demonstration of close engagement with the text Quality of analysis and interpretation Understanding of connections between the text and course themes Breadth and depth of knowledge displayed Communication (Eloquence & Clarity): Clear oral expression and organization of ideas Effective use of relevant concepts, terminology, and examples Ability to construct and articulate persuasive arguments Overall coherence and engagement in scholarly discussion Note: This assessment evaluates your ability to think and communicate spontaneously about course materials, demonstrating both your understanding of the texts and your capacity to engage in scholarly discussion about course-related themes and concepts. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

You may be able to defer this exam .

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Week 1: Introduction to Empire & its Critics This introductory lecture will reflect on why it's important to study empire today and provide an overview of the course. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
Week 1: Introduction to Empire & its Critics This seminar introduces students to the course and some of its defining themes and questions. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

Week 2: Liberalism & Empire (Mill) This lecture focuses on two essays by the great political theorist of Victorian Britain, John Stuart Mill: 'A Few Words on Non-Intervention' (1859) and 'Civilisation' (1836). The lecture will reflect on how a political theorist best known for his robust defence of liberty could also defend imperial governance. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06
Week 2: No seminar No seminar this week.

Week 3: Early Modern Europe & the Other (Las Casas) This lecture focuses on Bartolome de Las Casas's Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies (1552), a withering critique of Spain's imperial conquests in the Americas. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Week 3: Liberalism & Empire (Mill) What does Mill mean by 'civilization'? How does he distinguish the 'civilized' from the 'savage' or 'barbarian'? What do Victorian debates about intervention tell us about the imperial use of violence and claims to civilization? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

Week 4: Early Modern Europe & the Other (Montaigne) This lecture focuses on Michel de Montaigne's essay 'On Cannibals' (1580-92), an early modern attempt to hold an ethnographic mirror up to Europe during a period of violent confessional conflict. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Week 4: Liberalism and Empire (Las Casas) What were the main elements in Las Casas's damning critique of the Spanish conquest of the Americas? How did he represent the native peoples and the Spanish conquistadores? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

Week 5: Early Modern Europe & the Other (Behn) This lecture focuses on Aphra Behn's short work of prose fiction, Oroonoko, or the Royal Slave: A True History (1688). The lecture contextualises the novella in the tumultuous political context of 17th-century Britain and the Atlantic slave trade. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Week 5: Early Modern Europe & the Other (Montaigne) How does Montaigne handle the dichotomy between nature and culture? What does Montaigne really think of Europeans and so-called 'barbarians'? What kinds of rhetorical strategies does he use to reflect on Europe/France and its others? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

Week 6: Early Modern Europe & the Other (Defoe) This lecture focuses on Daniel Defoe's famous adventure story of the castaway, Robinson Crusoe (1719). The lecture contextualises the novel in Britain's Protestant political culture, its maritime exploration and colonialism, and the violence of piracy and slavery on the high seas. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Week 6: Early Modern Europe & the Other (Behn) What does Oroonoko reveal about Behn's views on empire, race, and slavery? What role does gender play in empire? Is Oroonoko an allegory for the drama of British politics? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07
Mid-Semester Break No lectures or seminars this week.

Week 7: Anticolonial Resistance (Fanon) This lecture focuses on Frantz Fanon's searing critique of colonialism in The Wretched of the Earth (1961), a book considered by many to be a foundational postcolonial text. The lecture contextualises the book in the Cold War politics of decolonisation and the philosophy of existentialism. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Week 7: Early Modern Europe & the Other (Defoe) What does the novel tell us about British conceptions of itself as an empire? If Defoe's novel is an allegory, what does it allegorise? Providence, the Protestant work ethic, the advancement of learning, rugged individualism, or colonial power? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

Week 8: Anticolonial Resistance (Said) This lecture focuses on Edward Said's Orientalism (1978), one of the foundational texts of postcolonial theory. The lecture explains Said's theoretical arguments about the relationship between knowledge and power in the West's construction of the 'Other'. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Week 8: Anticolonial resistance (Fanon) How does Fanon present the relationship between settler and native? What is the role of violence in colonialism? Can violence be a redemptive force for the colonised? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

Week 9: Frontier Imaginaries (Conrad) This lecture focuses on Joseph Conrad's Heart of Darkness (1899). The lecture contextualises the novella in Conrad's personal experiences as a mariner and the colonial violence of the 19th-century 'Scramble for Africa'. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Week 9: Anticolonial Resistance (Said) What does Said mean by 'orientalism'? What does it tell us about the western imagination and Europe's intellectual culture? What is the relationship between orientalism and colonialism? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

Week 10: Frontier Imaginaries (Coppola) This lecture focuses on Francis Ford Coppola's film about America's experience in the Vietnam War, Apocalypse Now (1979), a film loosely based on Conrad's Heart of Darkness . The lecture contextualises the film in the Cold War, decolonisation, and America's imperial intervention and warfare in Southeast Asia. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Week 10: Frontier Imaginaries (Conrad) How does Conrad represent Africa and Africans? How does he represent Europe and Europeans? How does the novella present European colonialism? What does Conrad's story reveal about European claims to civilization? Where does the 'heart of darkness' reside? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

Week 11: Frontier Imaginaries (Scott) This lecture focuses on Indigenous author, Kim Scott's historical novel, That Deadman Dance (2010). The story of contact on the 'friendly frontier' at King George Sound in the early 19th century raises questions about settler-colonialism's devastating impact on the people, language, history, and culture of the Noongar people. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06

Week 11: Frontier Imaginaries (Coppola) Is Apocalypse Now a good adaptation of Conrad's Heart of Darkness ? Is the film a critique of war (the Vietnam War in particular), colonialism, or America? How does the film treat the nature/culture and civilized/savage dichotomies? How do the music and visuals enhance the film? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07
Week 12: Revision Revision lecture and preparation for Oral Assessment. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07

Week 12: Frontier Imaginaries (Scott) How does Scott characterise the frontier contact between Indigenous and settler communities? How does he represent the two communities? How does the encounter on the colonial frontier transform the founders' ideals and impact on the two communities? What does the story reveal about settler-colonialism? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07
Week 13: No Lecture
Week 13: No Seminar
